questions.prompt: Why do mature AWS deployments use many accounts (one per environment, team, or workload) rather than one large account?

choices.text: Blast radius and management. A single account means an IAM mistake, a misconfigured policy, or a compromised credential can affect everything you've built. Separating accounts by environment (dev/staging/prod) and by workload contains the damage — a bug in dev can't touch prod's IAM or resources. Beyond blast radius, multiple accounts give: clearer per-account billing breakdowns (each workload's cost visible), more service-limit headroom (most AWS limits are per-account), simpler permissions (broad access in your own dev account vs. fine-grained shared-account policies), and compliance scope reduction (PCI data in its own account, rest of company outside scope). AWS Organizations + Identity Center + Control Tower make multi-account manageable. The right number depends on company size — a small startup might be fine with 3-5 accounts; a serious company has 50-500.

questions.explanation: Multi-account is the right AWS pattern beyond trivial scale. Blast radius, billing clarity, and management all improve. Most teams that started single-account later wish they'd separated earlier.

questions.prompt: A developer commits AWS access keys to a public GitHub repo. Within hours, the account has $10,000 in unexpected EC2 charges. What went wrong and how do you prevent it?

choices.text: The root cause is using long-term IAM user access keys at all. Public GitHub bots scan for AWS-style credentials and use them to launch crypto-mining instances within minutes of exposure. Prevention has several layers: (1) Use IAM roles instead of users wherever possible — services running on EC2/Lambda/ECS get temporary credentials automatically, no keys to leak. (2) For human users, use Identity Center / SSO instead of IAM users with long-term keys. (3) For systems that genuinely need long-term keys (some CI tools), scope tightly and rotate frequently. (4) Use git-secrets or similar tools to scan commits before push. (5) GitHub's secret scanning automatically notifies AWS when keys leak, and AWS often disables compromised keys quickly. (6) Set billing alerts so unexpected charges trigger alarms within hours, not days. (7) Use SCPs to restrict region/service usage in case keys do leak. AWS will often credit fraud charges as a courtesy but recovery isn't guaranteed.

questions.explanation: Leaked AWS keys are one of the top sources of unexpected charges and security incidents. Modern practice is to avoid long-term keys almost entirely via roles + SSO. The defense-in-depth combination above prevents the most common scenarios.
